title: Why the brain stem response overrides rational thought every time

htmlContent:
The moment your brain stem hijacks everything

I was sitting in a coffee shop last week when a man behind me slammed his mug down on the counter. The sound wasn't loud. Not really. But my body reacted before my brain could form a single thought. My shoulders shot up toward my ears. My breath stopped mid-inhale. My eyes locked on the nearest exit. All of this happened in less than a second. And here's the thing: I wasn't in danger. I knew I wasn't in danger. But my brain stem didn't care what I knew.

That's the reality of living with a nervous system shaped by trauma. Your rational mind can be fully aware that you are safe. You can tell yourself the logical facts. You can repeat affirmations. You can breathe. And still your body will override everything you think you know. Because the brain stem doesn't listen to words. It listens to sensation, to vibration, to the ancient language of survival.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping this exact phenomenon. His work shows that the brain stem processes threat through a hierarchy of responses. First it scans for safety. If it doesn't find safety, it looks for danger. And if it detects danger, it activates survival circuits before your cortex even gets the memo. That's why you can be in a therapy session, logically understanding that your partner isn't your father, and still feel your chest tighten when they raise their voice. Your brain stem doesn't do nuance. It does pattern matching. And it does it fast.

Stay with me here. This isn't about weakness. It isn't about not trying hard enough. It's about neurobiology. The brain stem evolved over hundreds of millions of years to keep us alive. Your rational brain, the prefrontal cortex, is a relative newcomer. It's like a toddler trying to reason with a grizzly bear. The bear doesn't care about your logic. The bear cares about survival. And your brain stem is that bear.

Why your rational brain doesn't stand a chance

Think about the speed of neural transmission. A signal from your eyes to your brain stem takes about 20 milliseconds. A signal from your eyes to your prefrontal cortex takes about 200 milliseconds. That's a tenfold difference. By the time your rational brain has registered what's happening, your brain stem has already decided whether to fight, flee, freeze, or fawn. You're not choosing these responses. They're choosing you.

This is where Bessel van der Kolk's work becomes essential. In his research at the Trauma Center in Boston, van der Kolk showed that trauma literally reshapes the brain. The brain stem becomes hypervigilant. It starts scanning for threat even when no threat exists. It begins to treat neutral stimuli as dangerous. A certain tone of voice. A specific smell. A particular time of day. The brain stem doesn't distinguish between a real tiger and a memory of a tiger. It responds the same way to both.

I remember a client who couldn't sit with her back to the door in any room. She knew it was irrational. She knew no one was coming for her. But every time she tried to face away from the entrance, her heart would race, her palms would sweat, and she would feel an overwhelming urge to turn around. That's the brain stem overriding rational thought. Not because she was crazy. Because her nervous system had learned that safety required constant vigilance. And that learning was stored in her body, not in her thoughts.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine's book walks through how the body holds trauma and how we can work with those somatic imprints. It's not about talking your way out of the brain stem response. It's about letting your body know it's safe now.

The illusion of control and what to do instead

Here's what most people get wrong. They think if they just understand the trauma enough, if they process the memories enough, if they get the right insight, the brain stem will calm down. But the brain stem doesn't speak the language of insight. It speaks the language of sensation, of rhythm, of safety cues that are older than words.

I spent years trying to think my way out of panic attacks. I would sit there, heart pounding, telling myself all the rational things. I am safe. This is just a panic attack. It will pass. And none of it helped. Because my brain stem wasn't listening to my thoughts. It was listening to the tightness in my chest, the rapidity of my breath, the tension in my jaw. Those signals said danger. And no amount of rational thought could override that.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work explores how trauma gets passed down through generations, shaping your brain stem responses before you're even born. It's a different lens on why your body reacts the way it does.

What actually works is slower. It's less dramatic. It involves teaching the brain stem, over time, that it can trust again. This happens through repeated experiences of safety. Not through understanding. Through felt experience. You have to show your brain stem, not tell it, that you are safe now. And that takes patience. It takes repetition. It takes sitting with discomfort without trying to fix it.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana's book offers concrete exercises for working with the nervous system. It's about creating conditions where the brain stem can learn new patterns of safety.

Working with the brain stem instead of against it

I want to be honest with you. This work is not linear. You will have days where you feel like you're making progress, and then a single sound or a look or a memory will send you right back into survival mode. That's not failure. That's the brain stem doing its job. The goal isn't to eliminate the brain stem response. The goal is to widen the window of tolerance so that when the response happens, you can come back to regulation faster.

One thing that helped me was learning to track my body's signals before the full response kicked in. I started noticing the subtle signs. The slight increase in heart rate. The shallow breathing. The tension in my shoulders. These were early warnings. And when I caught them early, I could do something small. I could take a slow exhale. I could press my feet into the floor. I could turn my head slowly to orient to the room. These small actions send safety signals to the brain stem. They say, slowly and repeatedly, we are okay.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you just take a deeper breath? Did your shoulders drop slightly? That's your brain stem responding to the idea of safety. It's learning. It's always learning. And that's the hope. The brain stem can change. It can form new patterns. It can learn that the world is safer than it once believed. But it learns through experience, not explanation.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link). Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey's book shifts the question from what's wrong with you to what happened to you. It's a small shift in language but a massive shift in how we approach the brain stem.

The uncomfortable truth about healing

I'm not going to tell you that this gets easy. It doesn't. The brain stem response will always be faster than rational thought. That's the design. But what changes is your relationship to that response. You stop fighting it. You stop shaming yourself for it. You start treating it as information rather than as a problem to be solved.

When your brain stem hijacks you, it's not your enemy. It's your protector. It's doing what it learned to do to keep you alive. And that protector needs to be thanked, not silenced. It needs to be reassured, not argued with. Over time, it will start to trust that you can handle things. But that trust takes time. It takes consistency. It takes showing up, again and again, even when your body is screaming at you to run.

Wild, right? The thing that feels like it's breaking you is actually the thing that kept you alive. And the way forward isn't to override it. It's to befriend it.

Healing Journey Resources

  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of body-based trauma healing
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Mark Wolynn on inherited family trauma
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to nervous system regulation
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey on trauma and resilience
